heroOverview: Cyclophoria is a latent torsional deviation of the eye — a tendency for one or both eyes to rotate clockwise or counterclockwise around the visual axis when fusion is interrupted, with alignment maintained during normal binocular viewing by fusional vergence. Unlike the horizontal and vertical heterophorias, cyclophoria involves a rotational (torsional) misalignment that the visual system must continuously compensate for. Population data suggest cyclophoria is present in a majority of people in small amounts (typically under 3°), but clinically significant cyclophoria causing symptoms is far less common, affecting an estimated 1–3% of the population. When decompensated or when associated with oblique muscle imbalance, it produces torsional diplopia, tilted visual fields, and chronic head tilt — symptoms that are frequently misattributed to vestibular or musculoskeletal conditions before the ocular cause is identified. Diagnosis requires specialised torsional testing by a strabismus specialist or binocular vision optometrist.

keyStats.prevalence: Small cyclophoria (under 3°) is present in up to 60% of adults; clinically symptomatic cyclophoria is estimated at 1–3% of the general population
keyStats.demographics: Symptomatic cyclophoria is most often identified in adults aged 20–60 presenting with chronic head tilt, torsional diplopia, or unresolved asthenopia
keyStats.avgAge: No strong age predilection; decompensation often triggered by head injury, fourth nerve palsy, or ageing of fusional reserves
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global prevalence not recorded; estimated several million affected worldwide based on fourth cranial nerve palsy incidence data

definition: Cyclophoria (ICD-10: H50.54) is a latent torsional misalignment of the eyes in which one or both eyes have a tendency to rotate around the line of sight (the visual axis) — incyclophoria describes inward (intorsional) rotation of the upper pole of the iris, while excyclophoria describes outward (extorsional) rotation. As with all heterophorias, the deviation is held in check by the fusional vergence reflex during binocular viewing and is revealed only when fusion is interrupted. Cyclophoria falls under the specialty of ophthalmology, specifically strabismus and neuro-ophthalmology. The superior oblique and inferior oblique muscles are the primary torsional movers; consequently, cyclophoria is most commonly caused by superior oblique paresis (fourth cranial nerve palsy), the most frequent isolated ocular motor nerve palsy. Excyclophoria — the most common clinical type — results from weakness of the intorting superior oblique or overaction of the extorting inferior oblique. Incyclophoria is less common and may result from superior rectus or inferior oblique weakness. Small amounts of cyclophoria are physiologically normal; clinical significance arises when the deviation exceeds the patient's torsional fusional amplitude (typically around 5–10° for most adults) or is large enough to produce a head tilt that strains the neck. Acquired cyclophoria in a patient without prior ocular motility history can indicate fourth nerve palsy, skew deviation, trochlear injury, or other neurological disease.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; the upper pole of the iris drifts outward (temporally) when fusion is suspended. Associated with superior oblique paresis or inferior oblique overaction. Produces a compensatory contralateral head tilt.
The upper pole of the iris drifts inward (nasally); less common, associated with superior rectus weakness or inferior oblique underaction.

Present from birth or early childhood, often associated with congenital superior oblique palsy; may be compensated for decades before symptoms emerge and a large vertical fusional amplitude develops as an adaptation.

Develops in adulthood due to head trauma, trochlear neuropathy, orbital disease, or neurological conditions; typically presents acutely with torsional diplopia and is more symptomatic than congenital forms.

primarySymptoms: Torsional (rotary) diplopia — double images that appear tilted relative to each other rather than simply side-by-side or above-and-below, the hallmark symptom of cyclophoria decompensation.
Chronic head tilt to one side, often away from the affected eye, adopted unconsciously to reduce the torsional misalignment and eliminate diplopia.
Eyestrain and fatigue after reading or sustained close work, caused by the continuous fusional effort required to compensate for the rotational deviation.
Headaches centered over the brow, temple, or neck, often arising from both ocular muscle effort and secondary cervical muscle strain from prolonged head tilt.
Vertical diplopia accompanying torsional diplopia in many patients, since superior oblique palsy (the most common cause) also produces a vertical deviation.
Difficulty with fine spatial tasks — reading maps, following ball sports, or any activity requiring precise torsional alignment of the visual axes.
Neck stiffness or cervical muscle pain on the side of the head tilt, frequently leading to musculoskeletal misdiagnosis before the ocular cause is identified.
Image tilt — the perception that vertical lines or the horizon appear slanted, particularly during monocular viewing or when the head is held erect.
Nausea or vestibular-like dizziness associated with torsional misalignment, particularly in acquired cases where the vestibulo-ocular reflex is also disrupted.
Visual confusion in complex spatial environments (shopping centres, crowds) due to conflicting torsional cues between the two eyes.
earlyWarningSigns: A mild preference for tilting the head to one side during reading or near tasks without an obvious musculoskeletal cause.
Intermittent difficulty reading when the head is held perfectly upright — letters or lines appearing slightly tilted.
Brief episodes of vertical or tilted double vision triggered by extreme fatigue, illness, or looking sharply to one side.
A persistent sense that the visual world is slightly off-horizontal in one eye when the other is covered.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of torsional or vertical diplopia in an adult with no previous history — this requires urgent assessment to exclude an intracranial mass, superior orbital fissure syndrome, or early brainstem lesion.
Cyclophoria accompanied by other neurological symptoms — facial numbness, limb weakness, difficulty swallowing, or altered consciousness — demands immediate emergency evaluation.
New head tilt following head trauma, even apparently minor, which may indicate a fourth nerve palsy requiring neuroimaging.
Cyclophoria in the context of orbital swelling, proptosis, or reduced eye movements — these suggest orbital disease or vascular lesion requiring urgent imaging.

causes.cause: Superior oblique paresis (fourth cranial nerve palsy)
causes.description: The fourth (trochlear) cranial nerve innervates the superior oblique, the primary intorting muscle; weakness or palsy allows the inferior oblique to dominate, producing excyclophoria. This is the single most common cause of clinically significant cyclophoria, accounting for approximately 30–40% of acquired vertical strabismus.

causes.cause: Congenital superior oblique laxity or abnormal insertion
causes.description: Developmental anomalies in the superior oblique tendon — including the Brown syndrome locking mechanism — alter the torsional force vector and produce congenital cyclophoria that may be compensated for years before decompensating in adulthood under increased demands.

The fourth nerve has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve and is susceptible to contrecoup injury from closed head trauma; bilateral fourth nerve palsies are particularly associated with head injury and produce large excyclophoria.

Congenital or long-standing cyclophoria with large fusional amplitudes can decompensate as fusional reserves decline with age, systemic illness, or sustained near-work demands, unmasking symptoms that were previously absent.

A supranuclear misalignment of the vertical and torsional axes caused by brainstem or cerebellar pathology; the torsional component produces cyclophoria-like symptoms alongside vertical diplopia and is identified by the pattern of ocular torsion being opposite to that expected in a trochlear palsy.

Orbital fractures (particularly blow-out fractures involving the orbital floor), orbital inflammatory disease, and thyroid eye disease can mechanically alter the path or action of the oblique muscles, changing their torsional vector and producing acquired cyclophoria.

Small cyclophoria (under 3°) is present in the majority of adults without any identifiable structural cause; it is considered a normal variation of ocular alignment that remains asymptomatic as long as fusional reserves are adequate.

riskFactors.description: Any significant blow to the head, particularly frontal impact, risks trochlear nerve contusion; bilateral fourth nerve palsy is a near-pathognomonic finding after severe head injury and produces a large excyclophoria.

Present from birth in approximately 1 in 400–500 live births; produces large torsional fusional amplitudes as an adaptation. Adults with undetected congenital palsy may decompensate under stress, illness, or presbyopia.
Torsional fusional amplitudes decline from the fifth decade; previously compensated cyclophoria can become symptomatic in middle or older age without any structural change in the deviation itself.
Microvascular ischemia is a leading cause of acquired fourth nerve palsy, accounting for approximately 35–40% of adult cases; glycaemic control reduces but does not eliminate this risk.
Arterial hypertension is an independent risk factor for microvascular fourth nerve palsy; controlling blood pressure reduces risk of this acquired cause of cyclophoria.

Posterior fossa tumours, meningiomas near the trochlear nerve, and raised intracranial pressure (which stretches the fourth nerve) are rare but serious causes requiring neuroimaging in adults with new cyclophoria.
Sustained close work increases the fusional demands on the torsional vergence system; individuals with borderline torsional reserves may decompensate during intensive reading or screen work periods.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20–60 presenting with unexplained head tilt or reading difficulty
Patients with closed head trauma of any severity, particularly frontal impact
Adults with poorly controlled diabetes or hypertension developing microvascular fourth nerve palsy
Children with congenital superior oblique palsy who decompensate in early adulthood
Neuro-ophthalmology referrals from neurology where vertical and torsional diplopia is the presenting complaint
Patients previously diagnosed with chronic neck pain or 'torticollis' where an ocular motor cause has not been excluded
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing cyclophoria requires specialised testing not available in a standard refraction or basic eye examination. The gold-standard clinical test is the double Maddox rod test, in which red and white Maddox rod lenses are placed before the two eyes and the patient reports the angle at which a rotating rod must be positioned to make the two lines parallel and horizontal — this directly measures the degree of torsional deviation in degrees. Fundal photography or direct ophthalmoscopy of the optic disc-fovea relationship allows objective measurement of ocular torsion; in normal eyes the fovea sits slightly below the lower pole of the optic disc, and torsion displaces it further. The Parks-Bielschowsky three-step test is essential when superior oblique palsy is suspected: it identifies whether the vertical element of the diplopia is in the right or left eye, whether it is worse in right or left gaze, and whether it is worse with right or left head tilt, systematically implicating one oblique muscle. Cover testing with prism measures any associated vertical phoria or tropia. Binocular visual field testing (Goldmann or Humphrey with fixation monitoring) can demonstrate the retinal correspondence mismatch produced by torsion. Neuroimaging with MRI of the brain and orbits is indicated in all adult-onset cases without a clear traumatic or microvascular cause. Specialist strabismus or neuro-ophthalmology assessment is usually required to integrate all these findings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures the degree of torsional deviation in degrees by asking the patient to align two coloured streaks produced by Maddox rod lenses placed before each eye.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You look through two coloured cylindrical lenses (one red, one white) placed before your eyes and rotate a knob until the two lines of light appear parallel and level; the angle of rotation reads the cyclophoria in degrees. The test is painless and takes about 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies which oblique or vertical rectus muscle is responsible for a combined vertical-torsional deviation, essential for diagnosing superior oblique palsy as the cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner covers each eye in turn while you look straight, then to each side, then tilt your head to each shoulder; the pattern of deviation and cover response across these nine positions implicates a specific muscle.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objectively measures torsion of the globe by photographing the position of the fovea relative to the optic disc; torsion greater than 10° from normal is pathological.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dilating drops may be instilled; the examiner photographs the back of each eye and measures the fovea-disc angle on the image. The procedure takes 20–30 minutes including dilation.

diagnosticTests.test: Cover test with vertical prism measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies any associated vertical phoria or tropia that accompanies the torsional deviation, guiding both surgical and prism planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Similar to a standard cover test — the examiner moves a cover between your eyes while introducing prisms of increasing power until no eye movement is seen on uncovering.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes structural causes of acquired fourth nerve palsy or cyclophoria including posterior fossa tumours, orbital disease, and brainstem lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive magnetic scan of the head taking 20–45 minutes; no radiation exposure. Gadolinium contrast may be used. You lie still in the scanner bore.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents any torsion-related retinal correspondence anomaly and provides a functional map of visual field overlap relevant to diplopia management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You press a button each time you see a flash of light while fixating centrally; each eye is tested separately and then together. The test takes 15–20 minutes per eye.

treatmentOverview: Management of cyclophoria depends on the underlying cause, the magnitude of deviation, and whether the deviation is stable. Acquired fourth nerve palsy from a microvascular cause (diabetes, hypertension) typically resolves spontaneously within 3–6 months in 75–80% of patients, and patching one eye during the recovery period is the primary intervention to relieve diplopia. Prism correction for cyclophoria is technically more complex than for horizontal or vertical deviations because standard prism glasses correct horizontal and vertical deviations well but cannot directly correct torsional misalignment; however, vertical prism can indirectly reduce the vertical component of a combined superior oblique palsy, lessening overall diplopia burden. Fresnel press-on prisms allow trial correction before grinding permanent prisms. Botulinum toxin injection into the inferior oblique muscle has been used as a non-surgical treatment for excyclophoria from superior oblique palsy, providing temporary alignment improvement while awaiting spontaneous recovery or before surgical planning. When the deviation is large, stable, and non-resolving, surgical correction — most commonly superior oblique tuck (shortening the tendon) or inferior oblique weakening (myectomy or recession) — is the definitive treatment. The aim of surgery is to reduce the torsional deviation to within the patient's torsional fusional range. Vision therapy for torsional fusion exercises has a more limited evidence base than for horizontal vergence, but some patients with mild symptomatic cyclophoria benefit from supervised torsional vergence training.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation and monocular occlusion
medicalTreatments.description: Patching or fogging one eye eliminates diplopia during the recovery period for acquired fourth nerve palsy; approximately 75–80% of microvascular fourth nerve palsies resolve fully within 3–6 months without intervention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 75–80% of microvascular fourth nerve palsies resolve spontaneously; observation with occlusion is first-line for new acquired cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Vertical prism spectacles (Fresnel or ground-in)
medicalTreatments.description: Vertical prism in spectacles corrects the vertical component of superior oblique palsy, which reduces the associated torsional diplopia burden and is often sufficient to restore comfortable single vision during recovery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides functional relief in 60–70% of patients with combined vertical-torsional diplopia from fourth nerve palsy when the vertical component is the primary disability.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection (inferior oblique)
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of botulinum toxin into the inferior oblique muscle temporarily reduces its extorting action, partially correcting excyclophoria; effect lasts approximately 8–12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reported alignment improvement in 50–70% of treated eyes in case series, with effect duration of 8–12 weeks; used diagnostically and as a bridge to surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Torsional vergence therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Specialised vision therapy exercises using cyclovergence training devices, synoptophore with torsional targets, or afterimage procedures to expand torsional fusional amplitudes in patients with borderline compensation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence is limited to case series; clinically meaningful improvement in torsional fusion range has been reported in approximately 50% of motivated adult patients completing a supervised programme.

surgicalOptions.name: Superior oblique tuck (tenorrhaphy)
surgicalOptions.description: The superior oblique tendon is shortened (tucked) via a surgical suture, increasing its mechanical advantage and restoring its intorting and infraducting force; the primary procedure for superior oblique palsy with excyclophoria.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70–80% of patients achieve torsional alignment within fusional range postoperatively; a second procedure is needed in 10–20% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Inferior oblique weakening (myectomy or recession)
surgicalOptions.description: The inferior oblique muscle's extorting force is reduced by excising a segment (myectomy) or reattaching it further back (recession), correcting the excyclophoria and associated hypertropia from superior oblique palsy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successfully reduces excyclophoria and hypertropia in approximately 75–85% of cases in published series; often combined with superior oblique tuck for large deviations.

surgicalOptions.name: Vertical rectus muscle surgery (inferior rectus recession)
surgicalOptions.description: In skew deviation or combined vertical-torsional misalignment, vertical rectus recession adjusts the vertical component and can secondarily reduce cyclophoria, simplifying the residual torsional correction needed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Results variable and highly dependent on aetiology; best outcomes in stable skew deviation; approximately 65–75% within target alignment at 6 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tinted or polarised lenses
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients with intractable torsional diplopia not amenable to prism or surgery, tinting or polarising one lens reduces visual interference without full monocular occlusion.

alternativeTreatments.name: Head position training
alternativeTreatments.description: Physical therapy and posture retraining to reduce the compensatory head tilt, addressing secondary cervical strain; best used alongside — not instead of — ocular treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Synoptophore therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: A binocular vision training device that can present separately rotated targets to each eye; used by orthoptists to train torsional fusional reserves in motivated patients with small symptomatic cyclophoria.

whySeeSpecialist: Cyclophoria frequently evades detection in routine eye examinations because standard cover tests measure horizontal and vertical deviations but not torsion; specific tests such as the double Maddox rod are required. A strabismus ophthalmologist or neuro-ophthalmologist is essential for any case of suspected fourth nerve palsy, torsional diplopia, or new adult-onset cyclophoria to exclude intracranial pathology and guide treatment. Orthoptists with strabismus training are valuable for the testing and conservative management phases. Patients with unexplained head tilt or chronic neck pain that has not responded to physiotherapy should be referred for ocular torsion assessment before cervical surgery is considered.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a strabismus ophthalmologist or neuro-ophthalmologist with experience performing the Parks-Bielschowsky three-step test and double Maddox rod testing. Ask whether the practice has a synoptophore for torsional assessment and therapy. For surgical cases, ask specifically about the practitioner's experience with superior oblique tuck procedures — the outcomes vary significantly with surgeon volume. If a neurological cause is possible, ensure close collaboration between the ophthalmologist and a neurologist or neuroradiologist.
preventionStrategies: Control blood glucose and blood pressure to reduce microvascular risk of fourth nerve palsy — the most common preventable cause of acquired cyclophoria.
Wear appropriate head protection during activities with risk of closed head injury (cycling, contact sports, motorcycling) to prevent traumatic fourth nerve damage.
Maintain regular comprehensive eye examinations to detect compensated cyclophoria before it decompensates.
Report any new head tilt in a child promptly for ophthalmic assessment — early treatment of congenital superior oblique palsy prevents amblyopia and neck problems.
Manage fatigue and sleep adequately to maintain torsional fusional reserves in those with known borderline cyclophoria.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain an erect or slightly forward head position during sustained near work to reduce torsional demand.
Take frequent breaks (20-20-20 rule) during reading or screen work if cyclophoria causes fatigue during those tasks.
Use good ambient lighting to support binocular fusion during near tasks.
Report any worsening of head tilt or diplopia to the treating ophthalmologist promptly — particularly if systemic conditions such as diabetes are being managed.
Seek physiotherapy assessment for secondary cervical muscle strain from chronic head tilt, in parallel with ocular treatment.

recommended: A low-glycaemic diet with good blood glucose management to reduce microvascular nerve palsy risk in diabetic patients
Adequate B12 and folate to support cranial nerve function
Low-sodium, heart-healthy diet to control blood pressure and reduce vascular risk
avoid: Excess alcohol, which impairs fusional vergence and can unmask latent cyclophoria transiently
High-sugar, processed foods that worsen glycaemic control and increase microvascular risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular cardiovascular exercise is encouraged as part of vascular risk management — particularly for patients whose cyclophoria is driven by microvascular fourth nerve palsy. No specific exercise restriction applies to cyclophoria itself. Vestibular rehabilitation exercises prescribed by a physiotherapist may be appropriate for patients whose cyclophoria has a vestibulo-ocular component, such as skew deviation associated with peripheral or central vestibular disease.
dailyManagement: Wear prescribed prism spectacles or use a patching schedule as directed by the ophthalmologist.
Perform any prescribed torsional vergence exercises at the same time each day.
Monitor head tilt daily in a mirror — worsening tilt suggests decompensation and should be reported.
Keep follow-up appointments for fourth nerve palsy at the recommended 4–6 week intervals; most microvascular cases resolve within 3–6 months.
Alert the treating clinician immediately if new neurological symptoms develop — headache, limb weakness, facial numbness — which could indicate a serious underlying cause.
prognosis: The prognosis of cyclophoria depends entirely on its cause. Microvascular fourth nerve palsy — the most common acquired form — resolves spontaneously in approximately 75–80% of patients within 3–6 months once the underlying vascular event stabilises. Congenital superior oblique palsy, when identified and treated appropriately, has an excellent functional prognosis; surgery restores comfortable single binocular vision in 70–85% of operated patients. Idiopathic physiological cyclophoria with adequate fusional reserves is not progressive and requires no treatment. Cyclophoria from a structural intracranial cause depends on the primary diagnosis but may improve partially or fully with successful treatment of the underlying condition. Without treatment, large or decompensating cyclophoria can cause persistent diplopia, significant head tilt leading to secondary cervical problems, and functional limitation in reading and visually demanding work.
recoveryTimeline: Microvascular fourth nerve palsy: most patients experience gradual improvement over 6–12 weeks, with full resolution in 75–80% by 3–6 months. If no improvement by 3 months, neuroimaging is repeated to exclude a structural cause. Post-surgical recovery: alignment is assessable within 6–8 weeks; final refraction and prism adjustments are made at 3 months. Torsional vision therapy programmes typically run 12–16 weeks with weekly sessions and a home exercise component.
complications: Chronic cervical muscle strain and torticollis from prolonged compensatory head tilt — can persist even after ocular treatment if established.
Amblyopia in children with untreated congenital superior oblique palsy, due to suppression of the deviated eye during visual development.
Progressive decompensation — latent cyclophoria that breaks down leads to constant diplopia, restricting driving, reading, and daily function.
Misdiagnosis and delayed treatment — cyclophoria mimics vestibular disorders, cervical spine disease, and psychosomatic complaints, with mean diagnostic delay in published series of 12–18 months.
Surgical under- or over-correction requiring a second procedure — occurs in approximately 10–25% of cases, particularly for large superior oblique palsies.

supportResources.name: American Association for Pediatric Ophthalmology and Strabismus (AAPOS)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on fourth nerve palsy — the most common cause of cyclophoria — including symptoms, diagnosis, and surgical treatment.

supportResources.name: NEI — Strabismus information, National Eye Institute
supportResources.description: National Eye Institute overview of strabismus conditions including oblique muscle disorders.

supportResources.name: North American Neuro-Ophthalmology Society (NANOS) patient resources
supportResources.description: Specialist neuro-ophthalmology society providing resources on cranial nerve palsies and ocular motility disorders.

sources.title: von Noorden GK, Campos EC. Binocular Vision and Ocular Motility: Theory and Management of Strabismus, 6th ed. Mosby, 2002
Brazis PW. Palsies of the trochlear nerve: diagnosis and localization — recent concepts. Mayo Clinic Proceedings, 1993
Tamhankar MA et al. Adult isolated fourth nerve palsy: etiology and outcomes in 116 patients. Ophthalmology, 2013
Parks MM. Isolated cyclovertical muscle palsy. Archives of Ophthalmology, 1958
Buckley EG, Flynn JT, et al. Superior oblique palsy. Survey of Ophthalmology, 1993
NEI — Facts About Strabismus. National Eye Institute, NIH
WHO — Priority eye diseases: strabismus. World Health Organization

confusedWithConditions.name: Esophoria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Esophoria is a latent horizontal (inward) deviation measured by standard cover test; cyclophoria is a torsional rotation around the visual axis measured by specialised double Maddox rod testing, not the routine cover test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Exophoria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Exophoria is a latent outward horizontal deviation; cyclophoria involves rotational misalignment. Both are heterophorias but require different tests, produce different symptoms, and are treated with different prism orientations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diplopia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Torsional diplopia (tilted images) is the symptom of decompensated cyclophoria; diplopia itself has many other causes including cataracts, refractive error discrepancy, horizontal and vertical palsies, and retinal disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cyclotropia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cyclotropia is a manifest (visible) torsional deviation present during binocular viewing, analogous to exotropia versus exophoria; cyclophoria is the latent form that appears only when fusion is interrupted.

relatedConditions.name: Cyclotropia (left eye)
relatedConditions.relevance: The manifest form of torsional misalignment; cyclophoria that fully decompensates presents as cyclotropia — a visible, constant torsional deviation during binocular viewing.

relatedConditions.name: Exophoria
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are latent ocular deviations (heterophorias) held in check by fusional vergence; they frequently co-exist and may require combined management with both horizontal and torsional prism or surgical planning.

relatedConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: A near-vision binocular disorder that, like cyclophoria, presents with asthenopia and near-task difficulty; distinguishing them requires specific fusional vergence and torsional testing.

relatedConditions.name: Diplopia
relatedConditions.relevance: Torsional diplopia is the cardinal presenting symptom of decompensating cyclophoria and the main reason patients seek neurological or ophthalmic assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Esophoria
relatedConditions.relevance: Another form of heterophoria that can co-exist with cyclophoria, particularly in patients with complex strabismus or after head trauma that disturbs multiple ocular motor mechanisms.

faqs.question: What is cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: Cyclophoria is a latent torsional misalignment of the eyes — a tendency for one or both eyes to rotate around the visual axis — held in alignment by the brain during normal binocular viewing but revealed when fusion is interrupted. It is the torsional equivalent of exophoria or esophoria.

faqs.question: What causes cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is superior oblique (fourth cranial nerve) palsy, which may be congenital or acquired. Acquired cases are most often due to head trauma, microvascular disease from diabetes or hypertension, or intracranial pathology. Small cyclophoria with no identified cause is a normal variation present in most adults.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is torsional diplopia — double images that appear tilted relative to each other. Other symptoms include a chronic head tilt, brow or neck headaches, eyestrain during near work, nausea in acute cases, and difficulty with spatial tasks. Many people have asymptomatic cyclophoria that needs no treatment.

faqs.question: How is cyclophoria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires the double Maddox rod test, which measures the degree of torsional deviation in degrees. The Parks-Bielschowsky three-step test identifies the causative muscle when superior oblique palsy is suspected. Fundal photography can objectively document torsion. MRI is needed in all adult-onset cases without a clear cause.

faqs.question: Can cyclophoria cause neck pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. People unconsciously tilt their head to reduce torsional diplopia, and the sustained cervical muscle contraction from chronic head tilt produces neck stiffness and pain on the same side. This often leads to physiotherapy or even spinal investigations before the underlying ocular cause is identified.

faqs.question: Does cyclophoria go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Microvascular fourth nerve palsy causing cyclophoria resolves spontaneously in approximately 75–80% of patients within 3–6 months. Congenital cyclophoria does not disappear but can be managed. Large structural cyclophoria from trauma or tumour is unlikely to resolve without treating the underlying cause.

faqs.question: How is cyclophoria treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on cause and severity. Acute microvascular cases are patched and observed. Vertical prism spectacles reduce diplopia from the combined vertical component. Surgery — superior oblique tuck or inferior oblique weakening — is used for large stable deviations, achieving alignment in 70–85% of cases.

faqs.question: Can glasses correct cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: Standard spectacle prisms correct horizontal and vertical deviations but cannot directly correct torsional misalignment. Vertical prism in glasses can reduce the vertical element of a combined superior oblique palsy, which lessens diplopia. Specialised cyclodeviational prisms exist but are rarely used clinically.

faqs.question: Is cyclophoria the same as cyclotropia?
faqs.answer: No. Cyclophoria is latent — the rotation is not visible during binocular viewing and only appears when fusion is broken. Cyclotropia is manifest — the eye is visibly rotated even during normal binocular viewing. Cyclophoria can progress to cyclotropia if fusional reserves are chronically overwhelmed.

faqs.question: What is a fourth nerve palsy?
faqs.answer: Fourth (trochlear) cranial nerve palsy weakens the superior oblique muscle, the eye's main intorting muscle. This produces excyclophoria — an outward tilt of the upper iris — along with a vertical deviation and a compensatory head tilt. It is the most common isolated cranial nerve palsy causing ocular motility problems.

faqs.question: Can cyclophoria be caused by diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Microvascular ischemia from poorly controlled diabetes is one of the leading causes of acquired fourth nerve palsy, which produces excyclophoria. Good blood glucose management reduces this risk. Most diabetes-related fourth nerve palsies resolve within 3–6 months once blood glucose is controlled.

faqs.question: Does cyclophoria affect children?
faqs.answer: Congenital superior oblique palsy causing cyclophoria occurs in approximately 1 in 400–500 births. In children it must be treated early to prevent amblyopia. The characteristic finding is a large torsional fusional amplitude the child has developed as compensation, which allows them to maintain single vision despite a significant structural deviation.

faqs.question: When does cyclophoria need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is indicated when the deviation is large, stable, and has not responded to optical management; when congenital superior oblique palsy has decompensated; or when cyclophoria has progressed to manifest cyclotropia. Surgery aligns the eyes in 70–85% of cases. Acquired microvascular cases are observed for 3–6 months before surgery is considered.

faqs.question: Can vision therapy treat cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: Vision therapy for torsional vergence has a limited evidence base but may help patients with small symptomatic cyclophoria who cannot tolerate prism or are not surgical candidates. Supervised torsional vergence exercises on a synoptophore have improved torsional fusion range in approximately 50% of motivated adult patients in case series.

faqs.question: How does a head tilt compensate for cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: Tilting the head to the opposite shoulder from the affected eye uses the vestibulo-ocular reflex to generate torsional eye movements that partially cancel the cyclodeviation, reducing or eliminating diplopia. This adaptation can become habitual over years and may require physiotherapy to correct even after the eye has been surgically aligned.

faqs.question: Is new cyclophoria in an adult an emergency?
faqs.answer: New torsional or vertical diplopia in an adult with no prior history requires urgent ophthalmic assessment within 24–48 hours. While most acquired fourth nerve palsies are benign microvascular events, intracranial tumours, aneurysms, and brainstem lesions must be excluded with MRI. Any neurological symptoms (headache, limb weakness) demand same-day emergency evaluation.

faqs.question: Why is cyclophoria often missed?
faqs.answer: Standard cover tests detect horizontal and vertical deviations but do not measure torsional rotation. A double Maddox rod test is needed for diagnosis but is not performed in routine vision screening or basic refraction. Symptoms such as head tilt and neck pain are often attributed to musculoskeletal causes, delaying ophthalmic referral by months or years.

faqs.question: Can cyclophoria affect balance or cause vertigo?
faqs.answer: Yes. Torsional misalignment disrupts the vestibulo-ocular reflex and spatial orientation, producing a sense of visual tilt and sometimes vertigo or nausea, particularly in acquired cases. The visual tilt illusion — a perception that the world is rotated — is a characteristic symptom of acute cyclophoria from fourth nerve palsy or skew deviation.

faqs.question: What is excyclophoria versus incyclophoria?
faqs.answer: Excyclophoria means the top of the eye tends to drift outward (temporally), seen with superior oblique weakness. Incyclophoria means the top of the eye tends to drift inward (nasally), seen with superior rectus weakness. Excyclophoria is far more common in clinical practice because fourth nerve palsy is the most frequent isolated ocular motor palsy.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after surgery for cyclophoria?
faqs.answer: After superior oblique tuck or inferior oblique weakening, alignment is assessed at 6–8 weeks postoperatively. Final prism needs and spectacle prescription are confirmed at 3 months. Patients with residual torsional deviation after surgery may benefit from supplemental prism or a second procedure, which is needed in approximately 10–20% of cases.

metaDescription: Cyclophoria causes tilted double vision and head tilt from a latent rotational eye drift. Learn diagnosis, prism and surgery options. Doctor-reviewed.
